secrc__001_a.text:
Transforming care for people with peripheral artery disease through
community interventions
Please describe your research career to date (1116/1000)
I have a strong track record of high-quality interdisciplinary research in the
rehabilitation of long-term condition that impacts beneficially on clinical
practice and patient outcomes. My research on consequences and rehabilitation
of inflammatory conditions and has now broadened to include other painful
long-term conditions (e.g., peripheral artery disease (PAD)). My total grant
income is £11.8million (£8.8million since 2021), with £2.9million funding
pending.
My research is at the forefront of the national agenda to shift care into the
community. I led the team that identified the factors that influenced home-
based walking exercise and tested the first, home-based walking exercise
behaviour change intervention for PAD patients (MOSAIC1). This research was
published in JAMA 2022, showcased in a JAMA editorial and broadcast by
multiple mass media organisations. It informed international guidance1 The
feasibility and implementability of MOSAIC1 are now being investigated in
Scotland2 and informed an NIHR PGfAR application (Role: Chief

secrc__001_b.text:
Investigator,
stage 2, pending).
I led one of the first research programmes to identify poor sensorimotor
function, functional performance, and quality of life in adults with rheumatoid
arthritis (RA). I provided key evidence on the safety and effectiveness of
exercise for reversing these debilitating effects, improving patient quality of life
and participation. The impact of my research was recognised by the British
Society of Rheumatology (BSR Prize 2002) and Chartered Society of
Physiotherapy (Robert Williams Silver medal 2003). I designed clinically
applicable, novel interventions and led trials that showed the effectiveness and,
cost effectiveness of a RA upper limb exercise programme and early RA gait
rehabilitation3. My research informed guidelines and drove the integration of
exercise into the management of RA. It informed
 NICE guidelines (NG100)
 BSR guidelines for the management of pain in Inflammatory Arthritis
(2022)
 BSR guidelines for management of Foot Health in Inflammatory Arthritis
(2024)
The excellence and impact of my research was recognised when I was invited to
give a BSR ‘Jewels in Crown’ keynote (2015), received the European Alliance of
1

secrc__001_c.text:
Associations for Rheumatology (EULAR) research excellence award (2019) and
co-author the ‘Oxford Textbook of Rheumatoid Arthritis (2022).
My collaborations with European experts from over 44 countries have enabled
participation in some of the most impactful European guideline development
group4. I am lead author on international outputs that drive practice change
including researchers from USA, Sweden, Australia, New Zealand and Denmark5-
7. These partnerships have sparked research collaborations (NIHR150711) and
invitations to act as international PhD supervisor8,9. I lead partnerships that are
pushing forward the transition of care to community rehabilitation. I designed
and secured funding for a consortium of over 32 researchers, professional and
voluntary organisations to deliver the NIHR-supported Community
Rehabilitation Research Incubator. This enabled participation in the Community
Rehabilitation Alliance, a coalition of over 50 charities and professional bodies,
driving the pace of change in commissioning, planning and delivery of
evidence-based rehabilitation nationally. I am invited to work in partnership
with community and acute clinical service planners as part of our

secrc__001_d.text:
Integrated
Care Board research working group to develop research that addresses local
patient needs and priorities.
My research leadership is defined by my role as an academic leader in
rehabilitation research and as a pioneer of physiotherapy research.
 I am a panel member for NIHR Pre-Clinical Academic and practitioner
Fellowships, Wellcome Trust Global Health Doctoral Fellowships, and
former member of NIHR RfPB, acting as lead assessor.
 As Chair of the D-MAPP programme committee [NIHR201612] and TSC/
DMC member for three trials, I provide strategic oversight of substantial
NIHR funding awards.
 In recognition of my expertise, I regularly review international grants
submitted on rehabilitation calls e.g. Dutch Physiotherapy Society.
 I was the first physiotherapist invited to be an Associate Editor for
Rheumatology Advances Practice and contributed to all aspects of the
editorial process including editing the inaugural special issue on ‘Exercise
as medicine: Physical Activity in Inflammatory Arthritis’.
 As Trustee and Chair of the British Health Professions in Rheumatology
scientific committee and member of the BSR conference committee I led
the research prioritisation, re

secrc__001_e.text:
viewed proposals, curated and showcased
high-quality research at the annual conference.
I lead flagship translational research capacity building activities. As a NIHR
Knowledge mobilisation Fellow, my team develop and deliver the national NIHR
2

secrc__001_f.text:
strategy for maximising research impact. I shaped the new NIHR application
form/guidance, developed researcher knowledge mobilisation capability
through a national webinar series and designed and delivered ‘maximising
impact’ training for NIHR RSS advisors. I will lead national translational
researcher capability as a founding member of the NIHR-supported Knowledge
mobilisation Incubator.
My substantial track record in developing researchers exemplifies my
interdisciplinary rehabilitation research programme. I developed and secured
funding for the NIHR-supported Community Rehabilitation Research Incubator
that builds researcher capability and capacity in multiprofessional community
practitioners. As physiotherapy lead for the HEE Integrated Clinical Academic
and NIHR Insights South-East London programme I promote research
opportunities to pre-registration students to inspire the next generation of
allied health professional researchers.
I effectively coach research students through fellowship applications,
researcher development, PhD examinations and postdoctoral awards. I’ve
supervised six PhD students to completion on time. I currently supervise three
PhD students and three clini

secrc__001_g.text:
cians completing NIHR predoctoral awards.
Through the BSR and NIHR mentoring programme, I mentor multidisciplinary
researchers at all levels. I recently coached one physiotherapy clinician to
successfully secure her first externally funded postdoctoral award. I mentored a
postdoctoral health psychologist to obtain her first tenured academic position. I
am now coaching her to supervise her first PhD student and develop her
advanced fellowship application. I mentor a medically qualified data scientist
and exercise physiologist NIHR Advanced fellows.
I am passionate about inclusive patient involvement and develop innovative
strategies to maximise involvement from underserved communities. I
established the City St George’s vascular public advisory group. By partnering with
patient and community groups (e.g., national PAD advisory group, Eastern
European and Romanian hub), our group now involves geographically diverse
patients from PAD underserved communities. Patients drive the identification of
important questions and partner us to deliver and disseminate impactful
research using creative strategies. To enhance involvement, I provide
technology support, plain and multiple language sum

secrc__001_h.text:
maries, research training
and reimburse expenses.
This NIHR Professorship will be a step change into leading multiple research
projects of international importance and a strategic leadership role aligned with
3

secrc__001_i.text:
the NIHR aims and mission for high-quality community rehabilitation research.
This professorship will provide me with the support I need to develop my long-
term vision: to radically shift the scientific understanding, quality of care and
equity of access to community interventions for PAD patients. The financial and
institutional commitment offered by the scheme and City St George’s will enable
me to build my research group, increase research activity, support community
rehabilitation research capacity building and maximise impact.
4

secrc__001_j.text:
Contextual factors (480/500 words)
Delayed commencement of research career. As a clinical physiotherapist I
had limited opportunities to experience or contribute to research. I did not
publish any research. As rehabilitation research was in its infancy and
physiotherapy research roles/mentorships were limited, I was unaware of how
take the first steps into research. Despite this, I self-funded an MSc Sports
Science at Loughborough University and, after returning to full time clinical
practice for 5 years (with no dedicated research time), I obtained a small
Arthritis Research Campaign bursary (£6,000) to fund PhD fees.
Lack of postdoctoral research opportunities; I completed my PhD viva when
pregnant with my first child. Despite substantial efforts to identify suitable
research roles, I returned to clinical practice after parental leave, and did not
complete any dedicated post-doctoral research. I commenced an educational
position in a university teaching department with minimal protected research
time. This limited the development of research collaborations, opportunities to
(co) write research grants and publish.
Maternity leave and caring responsibilities. I have had two periods

secrc__001_k.text:
of
maternity leave (one year each). One immediately after I completed my PhD
and another after I had completed my first year as a (teaching) lecturer.
Part-time working and high educational/administration load. Due to caring
responsibilities, I worked as a part-time teaching lecturer (0.5 WTE,2002-2013),
then Senior Lecturer (0.6 WTE 2013-2021). The primary focus of my department
was delivering three teaching intensive allied health professional programmes.
Educational activities accounted for at least 60% of my part-time roles. I
delivered a substantial teaching load, led three teaching modules, chaired the
postgraduate physiotherapy programme board and was postgraduate research
co-ordinator/ Chair of the postgraduate committee. For one year, I was
Programme Director for the MSc in Advanced Physiotherapy (0.8 WTE, 80%
education). I had little research time or support, despite working in a research
intensive university.
To increase research time and support, I changed intuitions in 2021. City St
George’s provided more research time, development opportunities, leadership
training and encouraged me to apply for this award. I have increased outputs,
postgraduate students, research ne

secrc__001_l.text:
tworks and funding. Whilst my teaching
load is minimised, I am seconded part-time to NIHR as a Senior Research Fellow
(Knowledge Mobilisation) so my research time remains restricted (0.4 WTE).
5

secrc__001_m.text:
Together, these circumstances have substantially impacted my research
trajectory including number of outputs, PhD students, research income,
collaborations and the delivery of my ambitious research programme. This
award will provide be a step change for my career. It will release me from
teaching and NIHR secondment which inhibit my research and leadership
capability and enable me to accelerate my team building. The beneficiaries will
be patients, clinicians, the NHS and academic community. This application has a
secure foundation, with guaranteed deliverables, alongside explorative
transformative projects.
6

secrc__001_n.text:
Plain English Summary (595/600 words)
Why is this research needed?
Community rehabilitation improves people’s health and wellbeing and saves
money by treating people at home. But community rehabilitation is not
provided equally, and it is not known how to best to treat people with some
conditions or whether treatments are acceptable to patients in the community.
Improving equal access to effective and acceptable community rehabilitation is
central to the new government’s NHS plan.
One of the conditions that we don’t know how to rehabilitate in the community
is called peripheral artery disease. Peripheral artery disease is poor leg
circulation due to narrowed arteries. People from Black, South Asian or Eastern
European communities, those with diabetes or living in areas with
socioeconomic deprivation are affected more by peripheral artery disease.
Severe peripheral artery disease can cause constant leg pain, sores and
gangrene in the toes. This is called chronic limb threatening ischaemia. Most
people need their arteries unblocked in hospital and then need to self-manage
their recovery at home. This is difficult but there are no self-management
programmes to support people with chro

secrc__001_o.text:
nic limb threatening ischaemia to
recover and live well at home.
Some people with milder peripheral arterial disease experience leg pain on
walking (called intermittent claudication). Walking frequently is the best way to
improve this pain and reduce the risk of severe artery disease. It is hard for
people to walk on their own when they are in pain. New ways are needed to
help people walk regularly, improve their leg pain and do more activities.
Most people with peripheral artery disease are not active enough to reap the
health benefits. This increases their risk of getting other health problems. There
are many ways to help people to become active. One way is to use a
smartwatch that records how much activity is done and sends messages to
encourage people to be more active. Smartwatches can be used separately or
as part of an activity programme. But some older people find smartwatches
difficult to understand and use.
Aims
To work with patients, practitioners and service planners to co-design ways to
provide community rehabilitation for everyone with peripheral artery disease
and explore new ways to help people to be more active. People who are most
affected by peripheral artery dis

secrc__001_q.text:
What will happen?
I will build a team to
(i) summarise the evidence, interview patients and practitioners and co-
design a self-management programme to help people with chronic limb
threatening ischaemia live well at home.
(ii) look at the results from a study of an existing walking exercise programme
for people with intermittent claudication and use this information to adapt
this programme so it can be provided in the community.
(iii) interview patients and practitioners, co-design and user test a new
acceptable and usable treatment includes a smartwatch to help everyone
with peripheral artery disease be more active.
(iv) co-design a plan to implement these treatments in the NHS, if these ways
are shown to be effective and acceptable.
How have patients and communities been involved?
Members of the public and patients from different backgrounds chose this topic
and helped design this research. They helped write this summary. The public
advisory group will meet every 6-months and be involved in workstream
management.
Sharing this research
Patients, practitioners, service planners and voluntary and professionals’
organisations (like Circulation Foundation) will plan and help share ou

secrc__001_r.text:
r research
progress and findings, so it is available in the NHS and for the public. This will
include writing scientific reports; summaries for clinical guideline organisations
and service planners; giving talks; hosting meetings; designing infographics,
animations, websites and social media.
Scientific summary (500/500)
Provision of community rehabilitation is inequitable. Many interventions are not
evidence-based or acceptable. Rehabilitation of some high burden conditions,
such as peripheral artery disease (PAD), receive relatively little national support,
prioritisation or research funding, fuelling health inequalities.
8

secrc__001_s.text:
PAD affects over 237 million adults globally. One PAD symptom is intermittent
claudication (IC) although in its most severe manifestation, PAD leads to chronic
limb threatening ischaemia (CLTI). Over half of those with symptomatic PAD will
die, have an amputation or cardiovascular event within five years of diagnosis.
CTLI prognosis is extremely poor; commensurate with terminal cancer. The
economic burden of PAD is huge and rising.
PAD patients report dissatisfaction with care and improving outcomes in PAD
patients across the symptom range is the highest ranking research priority
among all vascular conditions. Providing PAD rehabilitation and supporting
self-management will save money. Yet, equitable access to community
rehabilitation is compromised by lack of acceptable, feasible, interventions that
are not designed with patients or community practitioners, restricted resources
and a high and increasing demand from a diverse patient group with complex
needs. Many people with PAD are also excluded from research and unable to
access or use new treatments e.g. digital interventions.
New scalable, co-designed, inclusive PAD interventions such as practitioner
supported community rehabi

secrc__001_t.text:
litation, self-management programmes and ‘next-
generation’ mobile‐based health technologies (mhealth) are promising. Yet,
evidence is mixed, none have been designed with PAD patients, or
commissioned.
The aim of this professorship is to transform the care for people with peripheral
artery disease through community interventions. Over five work packages, I will
lead research that will:
1. Maximise diverse and inclusive involvement, participation and engagement
of underserved groups with PAD
2a Through systematic review, qualitative and participatory approaches,
codesign an inclusive supported self-management intervention to improve
quality of life in CLTI patients. 2b. co-design a funding application to investigate
inclusivity, effectiveness and cost effectiveness
3a. Through secondary analysis of existing quantitative and qualitative data,
participatory approaches, co-refine and feasibility test an inclusive home-based
walking exercise behaviour-change programme for people with IC for delivery
by multidisciplinary healthcare and non-traditional healthcare exercise
providers in primary care and community services. 3b evaluate it in a trial
9

secrc__001_u.text:
4a Through qualitative approaches, co-design, refine and user test an inclusive
mhealth intervention for PAD patients to increase physical activity 4b. design a
study to test usability, inclusivity, clinical and cost-effectiveness
5. Drawing on data from workstreams 2-4 specify the implementation context,
outcomes and mechanisms and co-design tailored implementation strategies
for inclusive community PAD rehabilitation services
This ambitious programme of work strikes a balance between building on my
existing work, with guaranteed high impact outputs, and projects which
promise a step change in trajectory within five years. It will spearhead the shift
of PAD care into the community and develop research capacity in under-
represented disciplines.
Mitigating research and digital exclusion and involving patients and the public
are central to this research. Patients identified the research questions, helped
plan this research and will help deliver and mobilise research progress and
findings using co-designed, systematic, creative approaches.
Background (5447/5000words)
1. The problem
Community rehabilitation is person-centred, out-of-hospital, care tailored for
people with different co

secrc__001_v.text:
nditions and fluctuating needs. Globally, one in three
people require rehabilitation and ageing populations and increasing numbers
of people living with long-term conditions are fuelling demand10. In 2017, the
World Health Organisation recognised that many countries, including the UK,
are not providing appropriate and equitable rehabilitation nor are they
equipped to address future rehabilitation needs. Their call to action urged
countries to scale-up evidence-based community rehabilitation to address
health and social inequities as a critical health priority10.
In the UK, provision of community rehabilitation is inconsistent and many
interventions are not evidence-based, feasible or acceptable to patients11.
Rehabilitation of some high burden conditions, for example peripheral artery
disease (PAD), have received relatively little national support, prioritisation or
targeted research funding to date compared to other cardiovascular diseases
(e.g., coronary heart disease or stroke), fuelling health inequalities12.
Peripheral artery disease affects over 237 million adults globally and 15-20%
of the UK population aged over 55 years13,14. It frequently occurs alongside
10

secrc__001_w.text:
other conditions e.g., diabetes, high blood pressure or cholesterol15,16 and
disproportionally affects socioeconomically deprived populations, and South
Asian, African Caribbean, and Eastern European groups15,17-19. It causes
substantial disability, increased cardiovascular morbidity and mortality and is
associated with feelings of powerlessness, isolation and poor quality of
life14,16,20,21. Its economic burden is huge and cardiovascular-related healthcare
costs alone in England for people with PAD are £1.4 billion/year, with wider
economic annual costs approximately £4 billion22,23.
One PAD symptom is walking leg pain (intermittent claudication, IC) although in
its most severe manifestation, PAD leads to rest pain, and tissue loss (chronic
limb threatening ischaemia (CLTI))24. Despite optimal treatment, over half of
those with symptomatic PAD will die, have an amputation or cardiovascular
event within five years of diagnosis15,16,20,25,26. CTLI prognosis is particularly poor,
even after revascularisation; commensurate with terminal cancer27.
Improving PAD outcomes is the highest ranking James Lind Alliance
research priority among all vascular conditions28.
PAD care is delivered t

secrc__001_x.text:
hrough a vascular network of secondary care hub
hospitals supported by community and primary care spoke services. Guidelines
recommend medical optimisation, lifestyle modification, revascularisation/limb
salvage, community rehabilitation and home-based self-management support
(including personalised home-based exercise, digital interventions)29,30,31. Yet,
equitable access to personalised community rehabilitation that develops an
individual’s capability, opportunity and motivation to avoid deterioration in and
self-manage their symptoms and condition at home is compromised by lack of
acceptable evidence-based interventions and a high and increasing demand
from a diverse patient group with complex needs11,32. Many interventions are
not designed with patients or community practitioners and are not feasible for
practitioners to deliver due to restricted knowledge, skills and resources
(technology, time, space, training, staff)11.
Community rehabilitation for PAD is an area of historical under investment and
high research need.
Equity factors influence access to healthcare, health opportunities and
outcomes, but less than a quarter of people with PAD are screened for eligibility
to par

secrc__001_y.text:
ticipate in rehabilitation trials and PAD patients are typically excluded
based on at least one equity factor33,34. Yet, these subgroups may respond
11

secrc__001_z.text:
differently to community rehabilitation due to differences related to social,
environmental, physiological or disease states. So, evidence when available,
doesn’t always relate to those most in need. This is important because findings
from PAD trials and systematic reviews of trials inform practice guidelines and
service planning so excluding these subgroups may mean that community
interventions may not be as effective once implemented.
These gaps need urgently addressing, and subgroups with higher prevalence or
disease burden, or those who do not receive or cannot access similar levels of
services need to be included in PAD intervention development and research so
evidence-based community interventions can be implemented equitability and
at scale.
2. Importance
PAD patients report dissatisfaction with care35. Low engagement (<50%
enrolment) and high attrition (30% non-completion) in hospital-based PAD
rehabilitation is driven by social (e.g. family support), physical (e.g. pain), and
environmental factors (e.g. travel)36,37,38. Women, older adults, CLTI patients,
people from low socioeconomic or ethnic communities are disproportionately
affected by these barriers39,40. New scalabl

secrc__001_{.text:
e, co-designed, inclusive interventions
are needed such as practitioner supported community rehabilitation, self-
management programmes and mobile‐based health technologies (mhealth)41,42.
These approaches will save money as patients who feel most confident in
managing their longterm condition have 38% fewer emergency admissions and
32% fewer emergency hospital attendances than those who feel least
confident43.
Public advisors and practitioners support this research because it addresses
avoidable deterioration in PAD and health from inequitable NHS access. It will
widen access to community care and address key goals set out in the 2015
WHO Global Goals pledge (e.g., ensuring equitable access to community
interventions)32.
This research focusses on five James Lind Alliance PAD priorities; How can we a)
improve patient outcomes b) make it easier for patients to get help for this
problem? c) help better educate patients with poor leg circulation? d) improve
exercise provision and access? and e) what are the best ways to reduce leg pain
without an operation?28
It aligns with National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) [CG147]
research recommendation (optimal exercise thera

secrc__001_|.text:
py for PAD)29 and NIHR
strategic themes44.
12

secrc__001_}.text:
Crucially, it is congruent with the Department of Health and Social Care areas of
research interests (early action to prevent poor health outcomes, accelerating
the adoption and scale-up of innovation)45 , NHS digital health strategy46 and
recent Darzi recommendations: shift care to community settings; expand
digital services; promote prevention and minimise disease progression47.
3. Existing evidence
Comprehensive PAD rehabilitation requires a suite of evidence-based
community interventions considering the severity, complexity and
biopsychosocial impact of PAD and associated multiple comorbidities on
everyday life.
People with CLTI have severely restricted lifespan, high symptom severity and
co-morbidities resulting in poor quality of life and high social isolation, even
after revascularisation27. Systematic reviews support the effectiveness of short
self-management approaches in people with long-term and life limiting
conditions on quality of life and independence48,49,50. When people receive
personalised support sessions their emotional wellbeing improves because they
feel more in control of their symptoms and condition, have greater confidence,
and gain more knowledge and strat

secrc__001_~.text:
egies to manage everyday life48-50. Evidence
for self-management interventions also emphasised the need for
contextualisation to 1) specific challenges and complexity of CLTI and personal
and cultural characteristics 2) understanding the community setting and 3)
training which addresses practitioners’ knowledge, skills and attitudes51,52. No
community supported self-management interventions have been co-designed
with CLTI patients or commissioned in the NHS.
Guidelines recommend supervised exercise programmes as first-line treatment
for IC as evidence is insufficient to recommend home-based walking exercise,
given inconsistent trial results29,53. Systematic reviews concluded that home-
based interventions are inferior to hospital based exercise but better than
providing no or basic exercise advice54,55. Most studies were small, and
outcomes were very low or low quality. Heterogeneous intervention delivery
methods, settings, intervention components that do not include tailored
behaviour change techniques, exercise dose variability, impractical
interventions, inappropriate outcomes and/or poor treatment fidelity all
contribute to uncertainty about whether home-based exercise interven

secrc__001_.text:
tions are
effective.
MOSAIC1, a feasible and acceptable home-based walking exercise behaviour
change intervention, addresses these limitations (Figure 1)56-59. In an efficacy
trial, participants with IC increased mean 6-minute walk distance by a clinically
13

secrc__001_.text:
meaningful difference, compared with usual care57. MOSAIC1 needs adapting
for delivery by community providers to diverse patients across the UK. This
informs my NIHR PGfAR application (Stage 2).
Physical activity is a tertiary prevention in PAD that reduces the need for
revascularisation, and reduces post-revascularisation complications in those
requiring surgery, as well as the need for further complex and costly
interventions. Yet, less 50% PAD patients meet physical activity guidelines for
general cardiovascular health60,61 or for improving IC62.
The COVID-19 pandemic prompted community services to incorporate mhealth
to deliver care and offer preventative strategies such as promoting physical
activity. Yet, older people often experience digital exclusion limiting
engagement42. Whilst mhealth interventions increase physical activity in the
general population, the effects in PAD patients is unclear63. A Cochrane review
comparing mhealth interventions versus usual care, exercise advice or
supervised exercise included only four trials and found no clear evidence of an
effect of mhealth interventions on walking or other health outcomes in people
with IC41. All included studies had u

secrc__001_.text:
nclear or high risk of bias, excluded CLTI
patients or people after revascularisation, and two studies enrolled majority
white participants, limiting generalisability41.
Mhealth interventions are evolving and now combine consumer activity
trackers (i.e. Smartwatches) with ‘just in time adaptive interventions’ (JITAI)–
personalised motivational messages delivered in response to activity feedback
and practitioner support64. Trials incorporating mhealth interventions
alongside strategies to mitigate digital exclusion show promising results65.
Whilst a PAD message library has been developed, these are untested66. These
‘next generation’ mhealth interventions potentially provide an exciting, low-
cost, adaptable and scalable PAD treatment. If co-designed with PAD patients
and integrated with digital inclusion strategies, they could offer either a
standalone community intervention or be incorporated interventions such as
MOSAIC1 to support long-term self-management.
Why is this research needed now?
My research has started to influence practice and now needs expanding to
address two key underserved groups - those with CLTI and those experiencing
research and digital exclusion. It addresse

secrc__001_.text:
s a crucial unmet health need; the
avoidable deterioration in PAD and health from inequitable access to
community rehabilitation. It will provide new community interventions and
workforce training for integration into the PAD care pathway that can be
adapted for different contexts and populations.
14

Aim:
To transform the care of people with PAD across the symptom range
through community interventions
Objectives:
1. Maximise diverse and inclusive involvement, participation and engagement
of underserved groups with PAD in all workstreams
2a Through systematic review, qualitative and participatory approaches,
codesign an inclusive supported self-management intervention to improve
quality of life in CLTI patients. 2b. co-design a funding application to investigate
inclusivity, effectiveness and cost effectiveness
3a. Through secondary analysis of existing quantitative and qualitative data,
participatory approaches, adapt and feasibility test an inclusive home-based
walking exercise behaviour-change programme for people with IC for delivery
by multidisciplinary healthcare and non-traditional healthcare exercise
providers in primary care and community services. 3b evaluate it in a trial
4a Through qualitative approaches, co-design, refine and user test an inclusive
mhealth intervention for PAD patients to increase physical activity 4b. design a
study to test usability, inclusivity, clinical an

secrc__001_.text:
d cost-effectiveness
5. Drawing on data from workstreams 2-4 specify the implementation context,
outcomes and mechanisms and co-design tailored implementation strategies
for inclusive community PAD rehabilitation services
_________________________________________________________________________
5. Project Plan
With public advisors, I developed a programme theory that proposes the
context, activities, outputs outcomes and impact of this professorship (Figure
2).
This research is informed by key frameworks (e.g., Medical Research Council
framework67, INCLUDE frameworks68,69, Updated Consolidated Framework for
Implementation Research (CFIR)70, mHealth development and evaluation
framework71)
15

secrc__001_.text:
Workstream 1 Research Inclusion (1-60 months)
Question: What engagement strategies are needed to reach to people with
PAD, particularly those from underrepresented communities?
Aim: To maximise inclusive involvement, participation and engagement of
underserved groups with PAD
Inclusion in community rehabilitation research is at the core of the
professorship, guided the EqIA72 and INCLUDE frameworks68,69. In this cross-
cutting workstream will focus creative engagement activities PAD under-served
communities (i.e., socioeconomically deprived, or South Asian, African
Caribbean, Eastern European communities) so their unique perspectives are
heard and inform all aspects of this research.
Research inclusion strategies comprise:
 Appointing a dedicated co-ordinator to lead research inclusion and public
partnerships. They will focus on developing trust and understanding
between researchers and our underserved groups.
 Co-designing culturally appropriate engagement strategies,
interventions, resources and study documentation with diverse
communities to realise our vision for excellent and equitable
opportunities.
 Inclusive recruitment strategies that promote participation from
underser

secrc__001_.text:
ved, marginalised groups and those not usually recruited into
research. This will be achieved by developing creative and inclusive
communications with underserved communities and patient
organisations to share research details and support participation.
Approaches will be made through our co-ordinator, so organisations are
not overburdened with multiple requests.
 Rewarding and recognising involvement, participation and engagement.
 Working with geographically diverse Local Research Delivery Network
(LRDN) (e.g., urban, rural, north, south, coastal) and building engagement
strategies with primary care and community services that serve
underserved populations.
 Reviewing our participant demographic data from each workstream and
discussing how our strategies are being implemented and how
challenges will be overcome with public advisors. Additional targeted
efforts and strategies will be applied to increase representation, where
needed.
16

secrc__001_.text:
 Offering language translation, technical support, fees for software for
online meetings and offering alternative ways of engaging e.g.
telephone, receiving paper-based information
 Offering cultural competency and inclusive research training to all
research team members, recruiting staff and providers.

us groups (n=6-8 participants/group) or

Workstream 3 Supported home-based walking exercise behaviour change

This workstream is part of an NIHR PGfAR application (stage 2) and builds on
the MOSAIC1 trial (n=190, 6 sites in Southeast England).57
Question: Can a home-based walking exercise behaviour change programme

3.1 Aim: to identify core determinants of long-term walking exercise in IC patients
Design: Secondary analysis of MOSAIC1 trial process variables57
Methods: Mediation analysis of MOSAIC1 trial psychological process variables78,79
will identify determinants of long-term walking exercise informed by behaviour
change80 and implementation theories70

secrc__001_.text:
3.2 Aim: to Identify factors that influence walking exercise and implementation
barriers and facilitators from patient and provider perspective.
Design: Framework analysis of interviews from 20 MOSAIC1 trial participants
and 14 physiotherapists will identify critical factors influencing longer term
walking exercise behaviour change and factors that influenced intervention
delivery and barriers/enablers to implementation56,57.
Using findings from 3.1 and 3.2, with the public advisory group the modifiable
determinants will be prioritise using COM-B80, refine the MOSAIC1 programme

The feasibility of recruitment and randomisation was tested in MOSAIC1 trial57.

Participants and setting: 32 diverse adults aged over 50 years with IC from 6

Interventions: All participants will receive MOSAIC2. It is envisaged that MOSAIC2

Question: Can an inclusive mhealth intervention be co-designed to promote

4.1 Aim: to explore preferred activity, sedentary behaviour patterns, potential

4.2 Aim: to explore practitioners’ experience of mhealth interventions in community

Participants: A purposive sample of up to 15 multidisciplinary practitioners (see
2.2).
Methods: Two online 1.5 hour focus groups (6-8 participants each) will explore

4.3 Aim: To co-design an mhealth intervention, including a personalised message

Design: participatory co-design approach
Methods: Drawing on 2.3 and 4.1-4.2, 15 diverse patients/carers and 15

secrc__001_.text:

identify key provider training components.
4.4 Aim: To explore comprehension and perceived usefulness of messages
Design: Cognitive ‘think aloud’ interviews
Participants: Up to 10-15 diverse PAD patients (see 2.2).
Methods: Using a cognitive interviewing approach, participants read a set of
randomly selected messages and prompted to verbally express their thoughts,
interpretations and perceived usefulness of the messages66,69. Responses will be
analysed thematically using an adapted coding category69
4.5 Aim: To adapt and integrate the prototype mHealth intervention into an existing
digital platform
Design: Software engineering and system integration
Methods: The prototype intervention will be implemented using an existing
mHealth platform, e.g., HealthReact, developed by collaborators at the
University of Hradec Kralove, Czechia. This platform will continuously collect
and evaluate data from a linked validated consumer activity tracker (e.g.,

secrc__001_.text:
sitting or when the patient begins walking) or by the patient’s daily or weekly
activity level. The platform will allow configuration of triggering parameters,
e.g. duration between messages, number messages/day. monitor, automatic

Interface.
4.6 Aim: To refine prototype mhealth intervention
Design: Iterative refinement of mhealth intervention using plan-do-study-act
cycles
Methods: Up to 15 diverse PAD patients will use the prototype intervention for

feedback after messages) and qualitative feedback (accessibility? ease of use?
Timing and usefulness of messages?) will be obtained. The intervention will be

4.7 Aim: To assess the acceptability, inclusivity and immediate effect of finalised m-
health intervention
Design: prospective single arm study with embedded micro-randomisation and

over 4-months from 6 community sites (see 2.2).

scale88) and acceptability (acceptability of Intervention Measure, the

4.8 Aim: To develop a funding application to assess inclusivity, clinical and cost-
effectiveness of the mhealth intervention

secrc__001_¡.text:
Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have
been involved in developing this proposal (353/350 words)
The public have framed this professorship so that research inclusion at its core.
Regularly, over two years, I discussed my research with diverse PAD patients
and their carers in coproduction meetings that have shape the focus, research
questions, and plan. Nineteen people with lived experience of PAD, including
people from African Caribbean, South Asian, and Eastern European
communities and those with CLTI shared their experiences of living with PAD
and their treatment. To reach people from underserved communities who did
not want to join a group or those experiencing digital exclusion, I talked to
them individually via telephone or in-person.
They discussed the barriers to receiving rehabilitation in large hospitals (e.g.
time, travel) and how they wanted to receive rehabilitation/support locally that
was co-ordinated and responsive. They described how this research was ‘much
needed’ as this part of NHS care was ‘unacceptable and burdensome’ and they
asked for ‘better access’ to rehabilitation and ways to help them look after their
condition and symptom

secrc__001_¢.text:
s independently.
Patients with CLTI described having treatment in hospital and then ‘being left in
a vacuum at home’. They welcomed sessions with a provider to help them ‘make
the most of their lives’. People with IC told me that home-based walking
exercise was often ‘overlooked as a treatment’, but that specific walking advice
with support was needed. They liked the content and format of MOSAIC1 and
commented that having this intervention provided in the community with
‘enough time with the provider’ was key to helping them walk more. They didn’t
mind who treated them ‘just as long as they were well trained and supervised’.
All advisors with lived experience of PAD recognised that becoming more active
was important and wanted to explore the mhealth using activity trackers,
although some were nervous about this being the only treatment option. They
were worried about ‘everything going online’ and suggested developing an
intervention that was easy to use and had support ‘on the end of a phone’. This
advice changed the focus of this workstream. My advisors helped identify all
research questions and develop the research plan. Three advisors read the
public summary.

Leadership and Development (887/1000)
In 2017, I completed the King’s College London emerging leader programme
and, in 2021, the St George’s, future leaders programme, Tangible benefits of
the programme have been greater national and international research
collaborations and research leadership. My leadership development is
continuing in: health service research, community rehabilitation service
delivery, capacity development and management and leadership at an
international level.
Using Vitae’s Researcher Development Framework, I have developed a
leadership development programme, focusing on:
 Transition to senior strategic leadership and personal effectiveness: choosing
commitments wisely (Domain B, C)
 Maximising international networks, engagement and impact (Domain D)
 Consolidating and expanding knowledge (Domain A).
 Sustainable research capacity development (Domain D)
This professorship will be a career step change into international strategic
research leadership aligned with the NIHR aims for quality rehabilitation
research. It provides senior training, development opportunities to work with
local and national policy makers, fostering exciting new collaborations through

secrc__001_¨.text:
research visits and networking opportunities.
Over the next 5 years I will:
Amplify my senior strategic leadership and personal effectiveness: as an
international rehabilitation research leader that leads and influences others to
bring about change across boundaries, with the NIHR Future Focused
Leadership Programme (in year 1).
To consolidate my strong and dynamic research leadership, I will be mentored
by Professors Mallen (primary care), Prof Busse (clinical trials), Prof Modarai
(vascular) and Prof Jones (inclusive rehabilitation research, co-design). My
personal development will be further supported by quarterly remote/in-person
leadership coaching with Dr Carol Daniels, a coach with extensive experience
of developing female academic leaders, throughout the 5-year professorship.
The sessions will build personal effectiveness and leadership capability in key
areas e.g. nurturing and developing others; influencing, mobilising individuals
and groups; prioritisation and self-management; delegation and execution. This
will enable me to transition to a more senior role towards the end of the
professorship and understand the leadership needs of others in my teams.
31

International engagement, influence and impact: Leading at the level of professor
will require me to engage and influence many different individuals and groups
nationally and internationally. I plan to visit two leading PAD researchers: Prof
Mary McDermott (Northwestern University) and Prof Andrew Gardner (Penn
State College) in the USA (year 2). These researchers lead groundbreaking
inclusive community-based PAD studies and transformed clinical services by
introducing targeted interventions for underserved groups. Coproducing
research and learning from them will directly inform my inclusive PAD research
programme and spark new international collaborations.
To reinforce my knowledge and ensure that I remain abreast of current
developments in remote physical activity monitoring and mhealth, I will join the
Faculty of Physical Education and Sport, Charles University, Prague, Czech
Republic (in year 2). Dr Tomas Vetrosky, a new collaborator (workstream 3), is a
leading European researcher in mHealth and physical activity trials in people
with long-term conditions. Collaboration with his centre will include further
learning on ecological momentary analysis, digital regulations and will

To further my international research and leadership profile, and networks, I will
attend the Society for Vascular Surgery, World Physiotherapy, World Congress
on Rehabilitation, International Primary Care and Accelerating Health Equity
annual Conferences.
Building knowledge and skills: To complement my existing clinical research

(mentor: Busse) to learn about innovative rehabilitation trial designs and
identifying and developing inclusive, meaningful outcomes for community
rehabilitation trials. I will also schedule the 5-day course in advanced clinical
trial design (adaptive, platform, sustainable trials) at Birmingham University
(Year 3).
In pioneering the shift of PAD rehabilitation into community services, I will
participate in one-to-one coaching with Dr Catherine Huffington, Director of
Health improvement Southwest London ICB to strengthen my knowledge of
planning and commissioning community services.

Career intentions
This Professorship will give me an extraordinary opportunity to deliver
exceptional and much-needed community rehabilitation research to transform
the care of PAD patients. City St George’s has committed to continuing to
support me and my research beyond this award through access to training
opportunities for current and new researchers in my group and providing
administrative and infrastructural support. At the end of this award, my goals
are to
 be leading a multidisciplinary, collaborative team who will maximise
inclusive research and support the delivery and mobilisation of my
international, high impact research
 have developed inclusive PAD community interventions to transform
community services and built on through securing further programme
grant funding
 move from national lead in research and knowledge mobilisation in
practice to enabling high quality methodological research to be done at a
pace
 increase my national and international profile, enabling me to maximi

 have established a sustainable collaborative strategy and network to
nurture the future generations of community researchers at all career
stages
 be a strong candidate for NIHR Senior Investigator status
Mentors
Professor Fiona Jones (host mentor)
Professor Christian Mallen
Christian Mallen is a past NIHR Research Professor, NIHR Senior Investigator
and Director of the NIHR School for Primary Care Research. He is an academic
general practitioner with a portfolio of research focusing on improving the
diagnosis and management of musculoskeletal and rheumatological disorders
in community settings. He has a strong track record in multi-disciplinary
working and supporting career development, as past academic capacity
development lead for the NIHR SPCR and director of the Wellcome PhD
33

secrc__001_®.text:
Programme for Primary Care. His research group is multi-professional including
clinicians (physiotherapist, nurses, pharmacists, psychologists and GPs) and
methodological experts (clinical trials, epidemiology, data synthesis and social
sciences).
Professor Mallen’s mentorship will fulfil three essential roles in my future career
development.
Firstly, as a leading international expert in primary and community care, where
my research is focused, he will ensure my research remains relevant to the
needs of patients and primary and community care practitioners and is
developed to be applicable in that setting.
Secondly, his experiences of developing an internationally renowned
multidisciplinary programme of research and his leadership role with the NIHR
SPCR will provide a unique perspective on academic training and capacity
building which will support me to build a strong cohort of multi-disciplinary
researchers.
Thirdly, he will provide opportunities for formal collaboration with world-
leading general practice researchers, for example, Professor Paul Little,
University of Southampton

Professor Monica Busse
Professor Monica Busse is an applied health researcher and trial methodologist
based in the Cardiff University Centre for Trials Research, Director of the Health
and Care Research Wales Faculty, and current Health and Care Research Wales
Senior Research Leader (Senior Investigator equivalent). Her research seeks to
enable people living with chronic health conditions to sustain an active lifestyle
while staying connected and engaged in meaningful aspects of their lives. In
CTR, she is Chief Investigator (or co-CI) of a suite of innovative NIHR and
charity/government funded rehabilitation trials. Prof Busse is an accredited
executive coach with an interest in promoting research careers and developing
future research leaders and is ideally placed to mentor me as I move through
this professorship and programme of work.
Specifically, I will draw on her experience, knowledge and insight of developing
an internationally renowned multidisciplinary research programme when

others in my broader network
Together with Professor Jones, she will act as a role model and nurture my
development as a strong female research leader. Prof Busse is a vital member of

Professor Bijan Modarai

Heart Foundation Senior Clinical Research Fellow, current President of the
British Society for Endovascular Therapy and leads the King’s Health Partners
Cardiovascular Clinical Academic grouping. He is an academic vascular surgeon
whose work focusses on improving diagnosis and management of people with
vascular disease by developing novel diagnostics and advanced therapeutics.
He is an advocate of “team science”, leading translational programmes of

My Professorship will extend established and successful partnerships and
create important new collaborations.
I will forge new international collaborations in:
United States of America: Prof Mary MacRae McDermott, Northwestern
University's Feinberg School of Medicine and Northwestern Memorial Hospital,
Chicago. Prof McDermott and her team have undertaken the flagship HONOR
and GOALs trials into community supported home-based exercise/mhealth in
PAD. Prof Andrew Gardner, Department of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation
at Penn State College of Medicine, is an international researcher who led
seminal trials comparing revascularisation and exercise effects in PAD. His
current work investigates PAD sub-populations such as people with
PAD/diabetes and ethnic communities. By working alongside these teams, I will
be able to gain an in-depth understanding of the interventions, questionnaires
and data used current and other studies, which will inform workstream 1-2,
support work coming out of workstream 3-4, and will spark/lead onto other
avenues for international collaboration.
